critique.summary: Apple has announced Apple Business, a new all-in-one platform launching on April 14, 2026, designed to help businesses manage devices, communication, and customer engagement. The platform integrates mobile device management, business email and calendar services with custom domain support, and new advertising options on Apple Maps in the U.S. and Canada.
mainPoints: Apple Business combines device management, business email/calendar with custom domain support, and customer engagement tools.
Built-in mobile device management allows easy configuration, security, and zero-touch deployment for employees.
New collaboration tools include integrated email, calendar, and directory services to streamline business communication.
Starting summer 2026, businesses in the U.S. and Canada can create local ads on Apple Maps to increase discoverability.
Brand management features unify control over brand presence across Apple Maps, Wallet, Mail, and other Apple services.
Apple Business replaces Apple Business Essentials, Apple Business Manager, and Apple Business Connect, with data migration and free availability in 200+ countries.
Additional paid options include upgraded iCloud storage and AppleCare+ for Business.
critique.truthfulness: The article is a direct announcement from Apple, a reputable source, detailing the launch and features of Apple Business. The claims about the platform's capabilities, availability, and pricing align with Apple's typical product rollout and service offerings. The information is consistent with Apple's known business services and device management solutions, and no contradictory evidence was found in external sources. The article accurately represents the new platform and its features as officially presented by Apple.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential limitations or challenges for small businesses adopting the platform, such as costs beyond the free tier or technical requirements.
Privacy implications of the new advertising features on Apple Maps, while addressed briefly, could be further examined for user and business impact.
The transition process from previous Apple business services to Apple Business might require additional support or training not detailed here.
The availability of certain features may vary by country or region, which could affect global businesses differently.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Apple's new business platform, with users expressing mixed views on its pricing, features, and usability. Some highlight potential benefits for small businesses and privacy advantages, while others criticize Apple's account management and late entry into the enterprise market.

comments.author: meego
comments.text:
I recently tried setting Apple Business Manager for our ≈20 people SME.

The first step was "Domain Lock/Capture" which takes over all Apple accounts for a specific domain.

I've never had a worse experience from Apple.

The process is buggy, filled with foot-guns and dead ends. It expects huge amounts of work from users who have had their account for more than a few weeks and are expected to remove a lot of their personal data before their account can be migrated (e.g. do you know how to delete all your Health data?). The process is also impossible to cancel.

Phone support was par for the course, e.g. tickets escalated to the abyss, suggestions to restore workstations to factory settings, etc.

Be warned.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, experience-based critique of the Apple Business Manager onboarding process, highlighting real usability issues and poor support.

comments.author: martibravo
comments.text:
599$ serviceable MacBooks, easy to use MDM, Cloud, Email and Calendar and flat-fee AppleCare all baked in?

New businesses under 50 employees are going to eat this up like there's no tomorrow.

I'd be scared if I was certain Redmond corporation who makes their money on 365 and Intune.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully predicts strong adoption among small businesses due to competitive pricing and integrated services, reflecting a realistic market perspective.

comments.author: jryio
comments.text:
When Apple vertically integrates it works for them. All the way from the cloud to the OS to the hardware. Pretty sure this will beat out tools like JAMF on user privacy alone by running trusted MDM adjacent tools in kernel space.

Yes sure you can use a different tool for any of these, defaults dominate for the same reason Google pays ~15 billion to be the default search engine on iPhones.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly notes Apple's strength in vertical integration and privacy, which could be a competitive advantage over existing MDM tools.

comments.author: monegator
comments.text:
Will we be able to change our company details? A couple of years ago we changed the business name, so let's change it in the account for billing and such.

Not possible.

Ok, let's ask support what to do: the only thing we can do is create a new account, get the approval, etc. and then ask for a migration that may or may not be approved and may or may not end succesfully.

In the end we keep receiving the bills in the old name, then change it manually or append a note.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a practical limitation in Apple's business account management, based on personal experience, which is valuable for potential users.

comments.author: giobox
comments.text:
How does this differ from the existing "Business Essentials" tool? The landing page for each looks like much the same product, at least the MDM stuff does?

> https://business.apple.com/preview

> https://www.apple.com/business/essentials/
comments.analysis: Raises a valid question about product differentiation, indicating some confusion or overlap between Apple's business offerings.

comments.author: dehrmann
comments.analysis: A concise but accurate observation about Apple's delayed entry into the enterprise business platform market.

comments.author: jwlake
comments.text: A non-terrible MDM that actually works would be really nice.  The rest I doubt they get much traction on.  Gmail is too easy, Google docs and sheets if you don't need Microsoft is also way better than Apple's free apps.
comments.analysis: Provides a realistic assessment of Apple's challenges competing with established productivity suites and the importance of a reliable MDM.

comments.author: zzyzxd
comments.text:
This is interesting to me as the IT support for my family. I have been considering using MDM to provision Wi-Fi credentials and other device configurations. 3rd party solutions are a little bit too much for what I need.

Apple Business Essentials with AppleCare+ for 3 devices and 200GB iCloud storage is $19.99 per user/mo. That's the same price as AppleCare One alone.
comments.analysis: Shares a practical use case for Apple's business tools in a small-scale environment, emphasizing simplicity over complexity.

comments.author: bitpush
comments.text:
Who will Apple serve? Users, Apple or their partners?

It has always been Apple > Users > Partners.

There's a reason why Microsoft is still the king of enterprises. Anybody getting involved with this with Apple will deserve everything thats coming their way
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about Apple's enterprise focus, contrasting it with Microsoft's established dominance, which is a common perception.

comments.author: wereHamster
comments.text:
business.apple.com doesn't work in Firefox, it redirects you to https://business.apple.com/abm_unsupported_browser?reason=Br...

Fuck you Apple.
comments.analysis: While pointing out a browser compatibility issue, the comment is overly emotional and unconstructive, reducing its usefulness.
